examples.explanation: Day 0 (100): span=1 (no previous days). Day 1 (80): j=0, price[0]=100>80 → break, span=1. Day 2 (60): j=1, price[1]=80>60 → break, span=1. Day 3 (70): j=2, price[2]=60≤70 → span=2; j=1, price[1]=80>70 → break. Day 4 (60): j=3, price[3]=70>60 → break, span=1. Day 5 (75): j=4, price[4]=60≤75 → span=2; j=3, price[3]=70≤75 → span=3; j=2, price[2]=60≤75 → span=4; j=1, price[1]=80>75 → break. Day 6 (85): j=5, price[5]=75≤85 → span=2; j=4, price[4]=60≤85 → span=3; j=3, price[3]=70≤85 → span=4; j=2, price[2]=60≤85 → span=5; j=1, price[1]=80≤85 → span=6; j=0, price[0]=100>85 → break.

Day 0 (10): span=1. Day 1 (4): j=0, price[0]=10>4 → break, span=1. Day 2 (5): j=1, price[1]=4≤5 → span=2; j=0, price[0]=10>5 → break. Day 3 (90): j=2, price[2]=5≤90 → span=2; j=1, price[1]=4≤90 → span=3; j=0, price[0]=10≤90 → span=4. Day 4 (120): j=3, price[3]=90≤120 → span=2; j=2, price[2]=5≤120 → span=3; j=1, price[1]=4≤120 → span=4; j=0, price[0]=10≤120 → span=5. Day 5 (80): j=4, price[4]=120>80 → break, span=1.

approaches.name: Brute Force O(n²)
approaches.summary: For each day, iterate backwards through previous days to count consecutive days where the price is less than or equal to the current day's price. This is straightforward but inefficient for large inputs.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Brute Force Stock Span
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of stock span calculation using nested loops
ladders.explanation: The stock span problem requires calculating for each day's stock price, the number of consecutive previous days (including current day) where the price was less than or equal to the current day's price. The brute force approach solves this by using two nested loops: an outer loop iterates through each day, and an inner loop iterates backwards through previous days to count consecutive days meeting the condition. The intuition is straightforward: for each day, we look backwards day by day until we find a price higher than the current day, counting all days with prices ≤ current price. This works because the span definition requires consecutive days starting from current day going backwards. The algorithm: 1) Initialize result array of size n. 2) For i from 0 to n-1: a) Set span = 1 (current day counts). b) For j from i-1 down to 0: i) If price[j] ≤ price[i], increment span. ii) Else break (prices are no longer consecutive). c) Store span in result[i]. Time complexity is O(n²) due to nested loops potentially scanning all previous days for each day. Space complexity is O(1) excluding output array. Connection to L1: This uses the backward scanning subroutine repeatedly. Common mistakes include: forgetting to break when higher price found (incorrectly counts non-consecutive days), off-by-one errors in loop bounds, and not initializing span to 1. Real code pattern: def stockSpan(prices): n=len(prices); result=[0]*n; for i in range(n): span=1; for j in range(i-1,-1,-1): if prices[j]<=prices[i]: span+=1; else: break; result[i]=span; return result.

examples.trace: Day 0 (100): span=1 (no previous days). Day 1 (80): j=0, price[0]=100>80 → break, span=1. Day 2 (60): j=1, price[1]=80>60 → break, span=1. Day 3 (70): j=2, price[2]=60≤70 → span=2; j=1, price[1]=80>70 → break. Day 4 (60): j=3, price[3]=70>60 → break, span=1. Day 5 (75): j=4, price[4]=60≤75 → span=2; j=3, price[3]=70≤75 → span=3; j=2, price[2]=60≤75 → span=4; j=1, price[1]=80>75 → break. Day 6 (85): j=5, price[5]=75≤85 → span=2; j=4, price[4]=60≤85 → span=3; j=3, price[3]=70≤85 → span=4; j=2, price[2]=60≤85 → span=5; j=1, price[1]=80≤85 → span=6; j=0, price[0]=100>85 → break.

Day 0 (10): span=1. Day 1 (4): j=0, price[0]=10>4 → break, span=1. Day 2 (5): j=1, price[1]=4≤5 → span=2; j=0, price[0]=10>5 → break. Day 3 (90): j=2, price[2]=5≤90 → span=2; j=1, price[1]=4≤90 → span=3; j=0, price[0]=10≤90 → span=4. Day 4 (120): j=3, price[3]=90≤120 → span=2; j=2, price[2]=5≤120 → span=3; j=1, price[1]=4≤120 → span=4; j=0, price[0]=10≤120 → span=5. Day 5 (80): j=4, price[4]=120>80 → break, span=1.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Backward Consecutive Count
ladders.desc: Count consecutive previous elements ≤ current element until a larger element is found
ladders.explanation: This subroutine is the heart of the brute force approach. Given an array and a current index i, it counts how many consecutive elements from i-1 backwards are ≤ array[i], stopping when a larger element is found. The intuition: The stock span requires consecutive days, so we can't skip days. Starting from the day before current day (i-1), we check each previous day moving leftwards. If the price is ≤ current price, it's part of the span and we continue checking the next previous day. Once we find a price > current price, we stop because the consecutive chain is broken. The algorithm: 1) Initialize count = 0. 2) Set j = i-1. 3) While j ≥ 0 and array[j] ≤ array[i]: a) Increment count. b) Decrement j. 4) Return count. Time complexity is O(n) in worst case (when all previous elements are ≤ current). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L0: This subroutine is called for each element in the main problem. Common mistakes include: not checking j ≥ 0 first (index out of bounds), using < instead of ≤ (incorrect for equal prices), and not stopping when larger element found. Real code pattern: def countConsecutiveBackward(arr, i): count=0; j=i-1; while j>=0 and arr[j]<=arr[i]: count+=1; j-=1; return count.

examples.trace: Start: count=0, j=4. j=4: arr[4]=60≤75 → count=1, j=3. j=3: arr[3]=70≤75 → count=2, j=2. j=2: arr[2]=60≤75 → count=3, j=1. j=1: arr[1]=80>75 → stop. Return 3.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Consecutive Comparison with Break Condition
ladders.desc: The pattern of comparing current element with previous elements until condition fails
ladders.explanation: This core logic captures the essential pattern of scanning backwards through an array while a condition holds, and stopping when it fails. The intuition is that many problems require examining consecutive elements relative to a reference point. Here, the condition is 'previous element ≤ current element'. We start from the immediate previous element and move leftwards. As long as each element satisfies the condition, we continue counting. The moment we encounter an element that violates the condition (is > current element), we stop because the consecutive chain is broken. This 'scan until break' pattern is fundamental to the brute force solution. The algorithm: 1) Set reference = array[currentIndex]. 2) Initialize counter = 0. 3) Start from index = currentIndex-1. 4) While index ≥ 0 and array[index] ≤ reference: a) Increment counter. b) Move to previous index (index -= 1). 5) When loop ends (either index < 0 or condition fails), counter holds the result. Time complexity depends on how far we scan. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L1: This is the abstract pattern that the subroutine implements. Common mistakes include: incorrect loop condition order (should check index bounds first), forgetting to decrement index, and misunderstanding when to stop (stop at first violation, not continue). Real code pattern: ref=arr[i]; count=0; idx=i-1; while idx>=0 and arr[idx]<=ref: count+=1; idx-=1.

examples.trace: ref=4, count=0, idx=1. idx=1: arr[1]=3≤4 → count=1, idx=0. idx=0: arr[0]=5>4 → condition fails, stop. Return 1.
ref=5, count=0, idx=3. idx=3: arr[3]=4≤5 → count=1, idx=2. idx=2: arr[2]=3≤5 → count=2, idx=1. idx=1: arr[1]=2≤5 → count=3, idx=0. idx=0: arr[0]=1≤5 → count=4, idx=-1. idx=-1<0 → stop. Return 4.

ladders.title: Building Block — Iterating Backwards Through an Array
ladders.desc: Looping through array elements in reverse order from a given index
ladders.explanation: This building block focuses on the fundamental skill of iterating backwards through an array from a specific starting point. The intuition is that many algorithms require examining elements in reverse order, and this requires careful index management. Starting from index i-1 (the element before current), we move leftwards by decreasing the index by 1 each step until we reach the beginning of the array (index 0). The key is to ensure we don't go before index 0 to avoid out-of-bounds errors. The algorithm: 1) Start with index = startIndex (typically i-1). 2) While index ≥ 0: a) Process array[index]. b) Decrement index by 1. This creates a loop that visits elements in reverse order: startIndex, startIndex-1, ..., 0. Time complexity is O(k) where k is the number of elements visited. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L2: This backward iteration is the mechanism used to implement the consecutive comparison logic. Common mistakes include: starting from i instead of i-1 (includes current element), using > instead of ≥ in loop condition (misses index 0), and forgetting to decrement index (infinite loop). Real code pattern: idx=i-1; while idx>=0: # process arr[idx]; idx-=1.

examples.trace: Start: idx=2. Loop iteration 1: idx=2 ≥0 → process arr[2]=30, idx=1. Iteration 2: idx=1 ≥0 → process arr[1]=20, idx=0. Iteration 3: idx=0 ≥0 → process arr[0]=10, idx=-1. idx=-1<0 → loop ends.
Start: idx=0. Loop iteration 1: idx=0 ≥0 → process arr[0]=5, idx=-1. idx=-1<0 → loop ends.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Array Elements
ladders.desc: Comparing values at different array indices using relational operators
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves comparing two elements from an array using comparison operators like ≤, <, >, ≥. The intuition is that algorithms often need to check relationships between elements, such as whether one element is less than or equal to another. Given an array arr and two indices i and j, we compare arr[i] and arr[j] using a relational operator. For the stock span problem, we use ≤ to check if a previous day's price is less than or equal to current day's price. The operation is straightforward: evaluate arr[j] ≤ arr[i] (checking if element at j is ≤ element at i). This returns a boolean (true/false). Time complexity is O(1). Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L3: This comparison is the condition checked during backward iteration. Common mistakes include: comparing indices instead of values (arr[i] ≤ arr[j] vs i ≤ j), using wrong operator (e.g., < instead of ≤ for equal prices), and not handling edge cases like empty arrays. Real code pattern: if arr[j] <= arr[i]: # condition true.

examples.trace: arr[j]=100, arr[i]=80. Check: 100 ≤ 80? 100 is not ≤ 80, so false.
arr[j]=50, arr[i]=50. Check: 50 ≤ 50? 50 is equal to 50, so true.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Accessing Array Elements by Index
ladders.desc: Retrieving values from an array using integer indices
ladders.explanation: This is the most fundamental concept: accessing elements in an array using their index. An array is a data structure that stores elements in contiguous memory locations, each accessible via a zero-based integer index. The intuition is simple: if you know the position (index) of an element, you can retrieve its value. For an array arr of length n, valid indices are 0 to n-1. Accessing arr[i] returns the element at position i. For example, if arr = [10, 20, 30], then arr[0] = 10, arr[1] = 20, arr[2] = 30. Time complexity is O(1) for access. Space complexity is O(1). Connection to L4: Array access is needed to get values for comparison. Common mistakes include: using 1-based indexing (first element is at index 1, not 0), accessing out-of-bounds indices (e.g., arr[3] for length 3), and confusing index with value. Real code pattern: value = arr[index].

examples.trace: Array has indices: 0→5, 1→10, 2→15. Access index 1: arr[1] = 10.
Array has single element at index 0. Access index 0: arr[0] = 100.

approaches.name: Stack O(n)
approaches.summary: Use a stack to store indices of previous days in decreasing order of price. For each day, pop from the stack while the price at the top is less than or equal to the current price, then compute the span as the difference in indices. This efficiently finds the nearest greater element to the left.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Stock Span Using Stack
ladders.desc: Complete implementation of stock span problem using monotonic decreasing stack approach.
ladders.explanation: The stock span problem requires calculating for each day how many consecutive previous days had stock prices less than or equal to the current day's price. The stack-based O(n) approach maintains a monotonic decreasing stack of indices where prices are in strictly decreasing order. For each new day, we pop from the stack while the price at the top index is less than or equal to the current price, effectively finding the nearest greater element to the left. The span is then calculated as the difference between the current index and the index at the top of the stack (or the current index + 1 if stack becomes empty). This works because when we pop elements with prices ≤ current price, we're eliminating days that don't affect the span calculation - their spans would be included in the current day's span anyway. The stack maintains only relevant historical data, ensuring each element is pushed and popped at most once, giving O(n) time complexity. The space complexity is O(n) in worst case when prices are strictly decreasing. This approach transforms what seems like an O(n²) brute force problem into an efficient linear solution by leveraging the stack's LIFO property to maintain useful historical information while discarding irrelevant data. The key insight is that when we encounter a higher price, all lower prices to its immediate left become irrelevant for future calculations since this higher price will block them from contributing to spans of subsequent days.

examples.trace:
Day 0 (100): Stack empty → span = 0 - (-1) = 1. Push 0.
Day 1 (80): Stack top price 100 > 80 → span = 1 - 0 = 1. Push 1.
Day 2 (60): Stack top price 80 > 60 → span = 2 - 1 = 1. Push 2.
Day 3 (70): Pop while price ≤ 70: pop 2 (60 ≤ 70). Stack top now 1 (80 > 70) → span = 3 - 1 = 2. Push 3.
Day 4 (60): Stack top price 70 > 60 → span = 4 - 3 = 1. Push 4.
Day 5 (75): Pop while price ≤ 75: pop 4 (60 ≤ 75), pop 3 (70 ≤ 75). Stack top now 1 (80 > 75) → span = 5 - 1 = 4. Push 5.
Day 6 (85): Pop while price ≤ 85: pop 5 (75 ≤ 85), pop 1 (80 ≤ 85), pop 0 (100 > 85 stops). Stack top now 0 (100 > 85) → span = 6 - 0 = 6. Push 6.

Day 0 (10): Stack empty → span = 0 - (-1) = 1. Push 0.
Day 1 (4): Stack top price 10 > 4 → span = 1 - 0 = 1. Push 1.
Day 2 (5): Pop while price ≤ 5: pop 1 (4 ≤ 5). Stack top now 0 (10 > 5) → span = 2 - 0 = 2. Push 2.
Day 3 (90): Pop while price ≤ 90: pop 2 (5 ≤ 90), pop 0 (10 ≤ 90). Stack empty → span = 3 - (-1) = 4. Push 3.
Day 4 (120): Pop while price ≤ 120: pop 3 (90 ≤ 120). Stack empty → span = 4 - (-1) = 5. Push 4.
Day 5 (80): Stack top price 120 > 80 → span = 5 - 4 = 1. Push 5.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Finding Nearest Greater Element to Left Using Stack
ladders.desc: Implement the core stack operation that finds the nearest greater element to the left for each element.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine is the heart of the stock span algorithm. For each element in an array, we need to find the index of the nearest element to its left that is strictly greater than it. We maintain a stack that stores indices in decreasing order of their corresponding values. When processing a new element at index i, we pop from the stack while the value at the stack's top index is less than or equal to the current value. After this popping phase, if the stack is empty, there's no greater element to the left (we can return -1 as a sentinel). Otherwise, the top of the stack gives us the index of the nearest greater element to the left. Finally, we push the current index i onto the stack. The intuition is that the stack maintains a decreasing sequence of values - when we encounter a new value, any smaller values to its immediate left become irrelevant because this new value will block them from being the 'nearest greater' for future elements. This monotonic stack property ensures O(n) time complexity since each element is pushed and popped at most once. The space complexity is O(n) for the stack storage. This sub-routine directly feeds into the full stock span problem because the span for day i is simply i - (index of nearest greater element to left), or i + 1 if no such element exists (when stack becomes empty).

examples.trace:
Index 0 (100): Stack empty → result[0] = -1. Push 0.
Index 1 (80): Stack top index 0 has value 100 > 80 → result[1] = 0. Push 1.
Index 2 (60): Stack top index 1 has value 80 > 60 → result[2] = 1. Push 2.
Index 3 (70): Pop while value ≤ 70: pop 2 (60 ≤ 70). Stack top now 1 (80 > 70) → result[3] = 1. Push 3.
Index 4 (60): Stack top index 3 has value 70 > 60 → result[4] = 3. Push 4.
Index 5 (75): Pop while value ≤ 75: pop 4 (60 ≤ 75), pop 3 (70 ≤ 75). Stack top now 1 (80 > 75) → result[5] = 1. Push 5.
Index 6 (85): Pop while value ≤ 85: pop 5 (75 ≤ 85), pop 1 (80 ≤ 85). Stack top now 0 (100 > 85) → result[6] = 0. Push 6.

Index 0 (10): Stack empty → result[0] = -1. Push 0.
Index 1 (4): Stack top index 0 has value 10 > 4 → result[1] = 0. Push 1.
Index 2 (5): Pop while value ≤ 5: pop 1 (4 ≤ 5). Stack top now 0 (10 > 5) → result[2] = 0. Push 2.
Index 3 (90): Pop while value ≤ 90: pop 2 (5 ≤ 90), pop 0 (10 ≤ 90). Stack empty → result[3] = -1. Push 3.
Index 4 (120): Pop while value ≤ 120: pop 3 (90 ≤ 120). Stack empty → result[4] = -1. Push 4.
Index 5 (80): Stack top index 4 has value 120 > 80 → result[5] = 4. Push 5.

ladders.title: Core Logic — Maintaining Monotonic Decreasing Stack
ladders.desc: Implement and understand the monotonic decreasing stack data structure operations.
ladders.explanation: A monotonic decreasing stack is a stack where elements (typically indices) are maintained in strictly decreasing order of their corresponding values from bottom to top. The core operations are: 1) While the stack is not empty and the value at the top index is less than or equal to the current value, pop from the stack. 2) Perform the desired computation (in our case, find the nearest greater element). 3) Push the current index onto the stack. The intuition is that this stack maintains only 'relevant' historical data. When we encounter a new value, any smaller or equal values to its immediate left become irrelevant for future computations because this new value will dominate them. By popping these irrelevant indices, we ensure the stack always contains indices whose values form a decreasing sequence. This property is crucial for achieving O(n) time complexity - each index is pushed once and popped at most once. The stack essentially acts as a filter that removes obsolete information while preserving what's needed for future calculations. In the context of stock span, this decreasing stack ensures that for any new day, the top of the stack always points to the most recent day with a price greater than all subsequent days in the stack. This directly gives us the boundary for calculating spans. The monotonic stack pattern appears in many algorithmic problems including next greater element, largest rectangle in histogram, and sliding window maximum.

examples.trace:
Start: stack = []
Value 100 (index 0): Stack empty → push 0. Stack: [0]
Value 80 (index 1): Top value 100 > 80 → push 1. Stack: [0,1]
Value 60 (index 2): Top value 80 > 60 → push 2. Stack: [0,1,2]
Value 70 (index 3): While stack not empty and value at top ≤ 70: top index 2 has value 60 ≤ 70 → pop 2. Now top index 1 has value 80 > 70 → push 3. Stack: [0,1,3]

Start: stack = []
Value 10 (index 0): Stack empty → push 0. Stack: [0]
Value 4 (index 1): Top value 10 > 4 → push 1. Stack: [0,1]
Value 5 (index 2): While stack not empty and value at top ≤ 5: top index 1 has value 4 ≤ 5 → pop 1. Now top index 0 has value 10 > 5 → push 2. Stack: [0,2]
Value 90 (index 3): While stack not empty and value at top ≤ 90: top index 2 has value 5 ≤ 90 → pop 2; top index 0 has value 10 ≤ 90 → pop 0. Stack empty → push 3. Stack: [3]

ladders.title: Building Block — Next Greater Element to Left (Brute Force)
ladders.desc: Solve the simpler problem of finding nearest greater element to left using brute force approach.
ladders.explanation: This building block problem asks: for each element in an array, find the index of the nearest element to its left that is strictly greater than it. The brute force approach is straightforward: for each element at index i, scan backwards from i-1 down to 0 until we find an element greater than arr[i]. If found, return that index; otherwise return -1. This has O(n²) time complexity in worst case (when array is sorted in increasing order) and O(1) space complexity (not counting the output array). While inefficient, understanding this brute force solution is crucial because it reveals the problem structure and helps appreciate why the stack optimization works. The key observation is that for element arr[i], we only care about the first element to the left that's greater than arr[i] - any elements after that don't matter. This is what the stack optimization exploits by maintaining only relevant candidates. The brute force approach also helps understand edge cases: what happens when there's no greater element (return -1), what happens with equal values (we want strictly greater, so equal values don't count), and how to handle the first element (always -1 since there's nothing to its left). This problem directly connects to the stock span problem because span[i] = i - (index of nearest greater element to left), or i + 1 if that index is -1.

examples.trace:
Index 0: No elements to left → -1
Index 1: Check index 0: 100 > 80 → found at 0
Index 2: Check index 1: 80 > 60 → found at 1
Index 3: Check index 2: 60 ≤ 70 → continue; Check index 1: 80 > 70 → found at 1
Index 4: Check index 3: 70 > 60 → found at 3
Index 5: Check index 4: 60 ≤ 75 → continue; Check index 3: 70 ≤ 75 → continue; Check index 2: 60 ≤ 75 → continue; Check index 1: 80 > 75 → found at 1
Index 6: Check index 5: 75 ≤ 85 → continue; Check index 4: 60 ≤ 85 → continue; Check index 3: 70 ≤ 85 → continue; Check index 2: 60 ≤ 85 → continue; Check index 1: 80 ≤ 85 → continue; Check index 0: 100 > 85 → found at 0

Index 0: No elements to left → -1
Index 1: Check index 0: 10 > 4 → found at 0
Index 2: Check index 1: 4 ≤ 5 → continue; Check index 0: 10 > 5 → found at 0
Index 3: Check index 2: 5 ≤ 90 → continue; Check index 1: 4 ≤ 90 → continue; Check index 0: 10 ≤ 90 → none found → -1
Index 4: Check index 3: 90 ≤ 120 → continue; Check index 2: 5 ≤ 120 → continue; Check index 1: 4 ≤ 120 → continue; Check index 0: 10 ≤ 120 → none found → -1
Index 5: Check index 4: 120 > 80 → found at 4

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Stack Push/Pop with Index-Value Association
ladders.desc: Implement stack operations while maintaining association between indices and their corresponding values.
ladders.explanation: This fundamental operation involves working with a stack that stores indices, but we need to frequently access the values associated with those indices. The pattern is: we have an array 'prices' and a stack 'stack' that stores indices. When we need to check the value at the top of the stack, we use prices[stack[-1]]. When we pop, we remove the top index. When we push, we add the current index. The key insight is that storing indices rather than values gives us more information - we know both the value (via prices[index]) and the position. This is crucial for calculating spans later. The basic algorithm pattern is: initialize an empty stack, iterate through indices i from 0 to n-1, while stack is not empty and prices[stack[-1]] ≤ prices[i], pop from stack, then perform computation (like finding nearest greater element), then push i onto stack. This pattern ensures we always have access to both position and value information. Time complexity for these operations is O(1) per push/pop, and space complexity is O(n) for the stack. This operation is the mechanical foundation for the monotonic stack approach - without understanding how to properly associate indices with values in stack operations, the higher-level algorithms won't make sense.

examples.output: Stack before: [0,1] (values 100,80). After popping while ≤70: stack becomes [0,1]. After pushing: [0,1,2]
examples.trace:
Initial stack: [0,1] representing indices 0 and 1
Check stack top: stack[-1] = 1, prices[1] = 80
Condition: 80 ≤ 70? No (80 > 70), so don't pop
Push current index 2 onto stack
New stack: [0,1,2]

examples.output: Stack before: [0,1] (values 10,4). After popping while ≤5: pop index 1 (value 4≤5), stack becomes [0]. After pushing: [0,2]
examples.trace:
Initial stack: [0,1] representing indices 0 and 1
Check stack top: stack[-1] = 1, prices[1] = 4
Condition: 4 ≤ 5? Yes → pop index 1
Stack now: [0]
Check stack top: stack[-1] = 0, prices[0] = 10
Condition: 10 ≤ 5? No (10 > 5), stop popping
Push current index 2 onto stack
New stack: [0,2]

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Understanding Stack Data Structure
ladders.desc: Learn basic stack operations: push, pop, peek, and isEmpty.
ladders.explanation: A stack is a fundamental data structure that follows Last-In-First-Out (LIFO) principle. Think of it like a stack of plates - you can only add or remove from the top. The four basic operations are: 1) Push: Add an element to the top of the stack. 2) Pop: Remove and return the top element from the stack. 3) Peek/Top: Return the top element without removing it. 4) IsEmpty: Check if the stack has no elements. Stacks are typically implemented using arrays or linked lists. In algorithmic problems, stacks are incredibly useful for tracking state, reversing order, parsing expressions, and solving problems with nested or hierarchical structures. The key intuition is that stacks naturally handle 'most recent' information - whatever was added last is what we access first. This makes them perfect for problems where we need to find nearest greater/smaller elements, validate parentheses, or perform depth-first search. In the stock span problem, we use a stack to keep track of 'relevant' previous days - days whose prices might affect future span calculations. Understanding stacks at this basic level is essential because the entire O(n) stock span algorithm builds on these simple push/pop operations arranged in a specific pattern.

examples.input: Start with empty stack. Push 10, push 20, push 30, pop, peek, pop, isEmpty
examples.output: Final: stack empty, isEmpty returns True
examples.trace:
1. Start: stack = []
2. Push 10: stack = [10]
3. Push 20: stack = [10, 20]
4. Push 30: stack = [10, 20, 30]
5. Pop: removes 30, returns 30, stack = [10, 20]
6. Peek: returns 20 (top element), stack unchanged = [10, 20]
7. Pop: removes 20, returns 20, stack = [10]
8. isEmpty: returns False (stack has one element)
9. Pop: removes 10, returns 10, stack = []
10. isEmpty: returns True

examples.input: Start with stack [5,3,7]. Pop, push 9, peek, push 2, pop, pop
examples.trace:
1. Start: stack = [5, 3, 7]
2. Pop: removes 7, returns 7, stack = [5, 3]
3. Push 9: stack = [5, 3, 9]
4. Peek: returns 9, stack unchanged = [5, 3, 9]
5. Push 2: stack = [5, 3, 9, 2]
6. Pop: removes 2, returns 2, stack = [5, 3, 9]
7. Pop: removes 9, returns 9, stack = [5, 3]

testCases.input: Start empty, push 1, push 2, push 3, pop, pop
Start empty, push 100, push 200, peek, pop, peek
Start empty, push 7, push 8, pop, isEmpty
Start empty, push 1, push 2, pop, push 3, pop
Start [50], push 40, push 30, pop, pop, isEmpty

approaches.name: Dynamic Programming O(n)
approaches.summary: Use an array to store spans and iterate through the days, using previously computed spans to jump back efficiently when prices are less than or equal. This avoids re-scanning all previous days by leveraging stored results.

ladders.title: Full Problem — Stock Span Using Dynamic Programming
ladders.desc: Complete solution using DP array to compute stock spans efficiently in O(n) time.
ladders.explanation: The stock span problem requires computing for each day the number of consecutive previous days where the stock price was less than or equal to the current day's price. The naive approach would be O(n²) by scanning backwards for each day. The DP approach optimizes this to O(n) by storing computed spans and using them to 'jump' backwards efficiently. The intuition is that if we know the span for a previous day, and that day's price is ≤ current price, we can skip checking all days in that span since they must also be ≤ current price (by transitivity). We maintain a DP array 'span' where span[i] stores the span for day i. For each day i, we initialize span[i] = 1, then check previous day j = i-1. While j >= 0 and price[j] <= price[i], we add span[j] to span[i] and jump j back by span[j] (since all days in span[j] are ≤ price[j] ≤ price[i]). This jumping avoids redundant checks. Time complexity is O(n) because each day is processed at most twice (once as current, once in a jump). Space is O(n) for the span array. Connection to L1: This uses the key subroutine of computing span for a single day efficiently. Common mistakes include not handling the jump correctly (infinite loops if span[j] = 0) or missing the condition price[j] <= price[i]. Real code pattern: def stockSpan(prices): n = len(prices); span = [1]*n; for i in range(1,n): j = i-1; while j >= 0 and prices[j] <= prices[i]: span[i] += span[j]; j -= span[j]; return span.

ladders.title: Key Sub-routine — Computing Span for a Single Day with Jump Optimization
ladders.desc: Compute the span for one day using DP jumps to skip redundant checks.
ladders.explanation: This sub-routine focuses on computing the span for a specific day i given previous days' prices and spans. The goal is to find how many consecutive days before i have price ≤ price[i]. Instead of scanning each previous day linearly (O(n) per day), we use the DP jump technique. We start with span[i] = 1 (counting the current day). Then set j = i-1. While j >= 0 and price[j] ≤ price[i], we add span[j] to span[i] and update j = j - span[j]. This jump works because span[j] tells us how many days before j (including j) have price ≤ price[j]. Since price[j] ≤ price[i], all those days must also have price ≤ price[i] (by transitivity: if a ≤ b and b ≤ c, then a ≤ c). So we can skip checking them individually. The intuition is leveraging previously computed information to avoid redundant work. Time complexity for this subroutine is O(k) where k is the number of jumps, but amortized over all days it's O(1) per day. Space is O(1) extra. Connection to L0: This is the core loop inside the full solution. Common mistakes include not checking j >= 0 in the loop condition or incorrectly updating j (e.g., j -= 1 instead of j -= span[j]). Real code pattern: def computeSpanForDay(i, prices, span): span[i] = 1; j = i-1; while j >= 0 and prices[j] <= prices[i]: span[i] += span[j]; j -= span[j]; return span[i].

examples.trace: Given prices: [100,80,60,70,...], span so far: [1,1,1,?,?,?,?]. Compute for day 3 (price=70). Initialize span[3]=1. j = 2 (day 2, price=60). Check: price[2]=60 <= 70, so add span[2]=1 to span[3]: span[3]=2. Update j = j - span[2] = 2-1=1. j=1, price[1]=80 > 70, stop. Final span[3]=2.

Given prices: [10,4,5,90,120,80], span so far: [1,1,2,4,5,?]. Compute for day 5 (price=80). Initialize span[5]=1. j = 4 (day 4, price=120). Check: price[4]=120 > 80, so stop immediately. Final span[5]=1.

testCases.input: Day i=1, prices=[100,80,60], span so far=[1,?,?]
Day i=2, prices=[10,20,30], span so far=[1,2,?]
Day i=3, prices=[5,5,5,5], span so far=[1,2,3,?]
Day i=0, prices=[50], span so far=[?]
Day i=2, prices=[1,2,3,2,1], span so far=[1,2,?,?,?]
Day i=1, prices=[100,90,80], span so far=[1,?,?]
Day i=3, prices=[10,20,15,25], span so far=[1,2,1,?]
Day i=2, prices=[30,20,10], span so far=[1,1,?]
Day i=1, prices=[5,5,5], span so far=[1,?,?]

ladders.title: Core Logic — DP Jump Technique for Efficient Backward Scanning
ladders.desc: The insight of using stored spans to jump backwards instead of scanning linearly.
ladders.explanation: The core logic is the DP jump technique: when computing span for day i, instead of checking each previous day one by one, we use the span of a previous day j to skip multiple days at once. Specifically, if price[j] ≤ price[i], then all days in the span of j (i.e., days j, j-1, ..., j-span[j]+1) must have price ≤ price[j] (by definition of span). Since price[j] ≤ price[i], by transitivity, those days also have price ≤ price[i]. Therefore, we can add span[j] to span[i] and jump j back by span[j] to continue checking further back. This reduces the time from O(n) per day to O(1) amortized per day. The intuition is similar to path compression in union-find: we compress the check of multiple days into one operation. This works because spans are monotonic with respect to price comparisons. Time analysis: each day is visited at most twice (once as current i, once as a jump target j), so total O(n). Space is O(n) for storing spans. Connection to L1: This logic is implemented in the subroutine's while loop. Common mistakes include not understanding transitivity or incorrectly assuming the jump works for price[j] > price[i]. Real code pattern: while j >= 0 and prices[j] <= prices[i]: span[i] += span[j]; j -= span[j];.

examples.input: Jump from day i=5 with price=75, j=4 with price=60 and span=1
examples.output: Add span[4]=1 to span[5], jump j to 3
examples.trace: At day 5, price=75. Current j=4, price[4]=60 ≤ 75. Since price[4] ≤ price[5], all days in span[4] (just day 4) have price ≤ 60 ≤ 75. So we add span[4]=1 to span[5]. Then jump j back by span[4]=1: new j = 4-1=3. Now check day 3 next.

examples.input: Jump from day i=6 with price=85, j=5 with price=75 and span=4
examples.output: Add span[5]=4 to span[6], jump j to 1
examples.trace: At day 6, price=85. Current j=5, price[5]=75 ≤ 85. Since price[5] ≤ price[6], all days in span[5] (days 5,4,3,2) have price ≤ 75 ≤ 85. So we add span[5]=4 to span[6]. Then jump j back by span[5]=4: new j = 5-4=1. Now check day 1 next.

ladders.title: Building Block — Computing Span for a Day with Linear Scan
ladders.desc: Compute the span for a day by scanning previous days linearly without optimization.
ladders.explanation: This building block solves a simpler version: for a given day i, compute its span by scanning all previous days from i-1 backwards until we find a price greater than price[i]. The span is the number of consecutive days where price ≤ price[i], including the current day. So we start count = 1 (for day i itself), then for j from i-1 down to 0: if price[j] ≤ price[i], increment count by 1; else break. This is the naive O(n) per day approach that the DP method optimizes. The intuition is straightforward: we check each previous day in order until a higher price stops the streak. Time complexity for this block is O(n) in worst case (e.g., increasing prices). Space is O(1). Connection to L2: The DP jump technique improves upon this by avoiding the linear scan. Common mistakes include off-by-one errors (e.g., not counting the current day) or not breaking when price[j] > price[i]. Real code pattern: def linearSpan(i, prices): count = 1; for j in range(i-1, -1, -1): if prices[j] <= prices[i]: count += 1; else: break; return count.

examples.trace: Price[3]=70. Start count=1. j=2: price[2]=60 ≤ 70, count=2. j=1: price[1]=80 > 70, break. Final count=2.
Price[5]=80. Start count=1. j=4: price[4]=120 > 80, break immediately. Final count=1.

ladders.title: Basic Operation — Comparing Prices and Counting Consecutive Days
ladders.desc: Compare stock prices and count how many consecutive days satisfy a condition.
ladders.explanation: This basic operation involves two parts: (1) comparing two stock prices to check if one is less than or equal to another, and (2) counting consecutive days in an array where a condition holds (e.g., price ≤ current price). For comparison, we use the ≤ operator to determine if a previous day's price is not greater than the current day's price. For counting, we iterate through an array (e.g., previous days) and increment a counter as long as the condition holds; stop when it fails. This is fundamental to the span calculation: the span is essentially the count of consecutive days (including current) where all prices are ≤ current price. The intuition is that we're measuring a 'streak' of non-increasing prices relative to the current day. Time complexity is O(k) for counting k days. Space is O(1). Connection to L3: This operation is used in the linear scan method. Common mistakes include using < instead of ≤ (which would undercount equal prices) or not resetting the counter properly. Real code pattern: count = 0; for price in prices: if price <= target: count += 1; else: break;.

examples.input: Target price=70, prices to check=[60,80] (in order from recent to past)
examples.trace: Start count=0. Check first price=60: 60 ≤ 70, so count=1. Next price=80: 80 > 70, so stop. Final count=1.
Start count=0. First price=5: 5 ≤ 5, count=1. Second price=5: 5 ≤ 5, count=2. Third price=5: 5 ≤ 5, count=3. No more prices, so final count=3.

ladders.title: Concept Foundation — Arrays and Basic Loops with Conditions
ladders.desc: Access array elements, use loops to iterate, and apply conditional checks.
ladders.explanation: This foundation covers basic programming concepts: arrays (or lists) to store sequences of numbers (e.g., stock prices), accessing elements by index (e.g., prices[i] for day i), using loops (e.g., for or while) to iterate through arrays, and conditional statements (e.g., if) to make decisions based on comparisons. For the stock span problem, we need to traverse the prices array from left to right, and for each day, look back at previous days. This involves nested loops in naive solutions. The intuition is that arrays allow us to store and retrieve data efficiently by position, loops enable repetitive processing, and conditions let us filter based on price values. Time complexity basics: a single loop over n elements is O(n). Space is O(n) for storing the array. Connection to L4: These basics are used to implement price comparisons and counting. Common mistakes include index out-of-bounds errors (e.g., accessing prices[-1]) or infinite loops. Real code pattern: prices = [100,80,60]; for i in range(len(prices)): print(prices[i]); if i > 0 and prices[i] <= prices[i-1]: print('price not increased').

examples.input: Array [10,20,30], iterate and print each element
examples.trace: Initialize i=0: access prices[0]=10, print 10. i=1: access prices[1]=20, print 20. i=2: access prices[2]=30, print 30. Loop ends.

examples.input: Array [5,3,7], check if each element is greater than 4
examples.trace: i=0: prices[0]=5, check 5>4? True. i=1: prices[1]=3, check 3>4? False. i=2: prices[2]=7, check 7>4? True.
